title: How to Compost in an Apartment
excerpt: You do not need a backyard to compost successfully. With the right indoor method, apartment composting can be clean, practical, and easy to manage while helping reduce kitchen waste and support a more sustainable routine.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Small-Space Composting

Living in an apartment does not mean you have to give up composting. Even without a backyard, you can still turn food scraps into something useful and reduce the amount of waste you send to the trash.

Apartment composting works a little differently from outdoor composting, but it can be simple, clean, and practical when you choose the right method. With a small setup and a few good habits, composting indoors can fit easily into everyday life.

Can You Really Compost Without a Yard?

Yes, absolutely. Many people compost successfully in apartments, condos, and other small living spaces.

The main difference is that apartment composting usually uses a more compact and controlled system. Instead of a large outdoor pile, you use a contained method that suits indoor living.

This can help you:

  • Reduce kitchen waste
  • Create useful compost for houseplants or container gardens
  • Live more sustainably
  • Cut down on trash odors from food scraps

The key is choosing a method that matches your space and routine.

Best Composting Methods for Apartments

Not every composting method works well indoors. In an apartment, the best systems are usually the ones that stay compact, control odor, and are easy to manage.

1. Vermicomposting

Vermicomposting uses worms, usually red wigglers, to break down food scraps in a small bin. This is one of the most popular apartment composting methods.

It works well because:

  • It takes up little space
  • It is quiet and low-maintenance
  • It produces nutrient-rich worm castings
  • It can be done indoors year-round

A properly managed worm bin should not smell bad.

2. Bokashi Composting

Bokashi is a fermentation-based method that uses a special bran with beneficial microbes to break down food waste in a sealed bucket.

It is useful for apartments because:

  • The bin stays closed
  • It handles many kitchen scraps
  • It takes up very little room
  • It usually creates less odor than open composting

The material it produces is not finished compost right away, but it can be buried in soil or added to a larger compost system later.

3. Countertop Scrap Collection With Off-Site Composting

Some apartment dwellers collect scraps in a small kitchen bin and then transfer them to a community compost site, local drop-off point, or shared compost system.

This is a good option if:

  • You do not want a full indoor compost setup
  • Your city or building offers composting access
  • You want the easiest low-maintenance option

It still helps reduce waste without requiring you to manage the full composting process indoors.

What Can You Compost in an Apartment?

The best materials for apartment composting are usually simple kitchen scraps.

Common compostable items include:

  • Fruit peels
  • Vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Tea leaves
  • Crushed eggshells
  • Stale bread in small amounts
  • Paper napkins without chemicals

Exactly what you can add depends on the method you choose. Worm bins and bokashi systems have slightly different rules, so it helps to follow method-specific guidelines.

What Should You Avoid?

To keep apartment composting clean and manageable, avoid materials that cause odor or attract pests.

Usually best avoided:

  • Meat
  • Fish
  • Dairy
  • Oily foods
  • Greasy leftovers
  • Large amounts of cooked food

These items are especially risky in small indoor spaces unless you are using a system specifically designed to handle them.

How to Store Food Scraps in a Small Space

One of the easiest ways to make apartment composting work is to manage scraps neatly before adding them to your compost system.

Useful options include:

  • A small countertop compost container
  • A sealed container under the sink
  • A container in the freezer
  • A lidded bin lined with paper or compostable material

Freezing scraps is especially helpful if you do not want odors or fruit flies in the kitchen.

How to Prevent Smells

Odor is one of the biggest worries people have about apartment composting. Fortunately, a well-managed indoor compost setup should not smell strongly.

To keep odors under control:

  • Use a sealed or well-designed bin
  • Avoid overloading the system
  • Do not add greasy or unsuitable foods
  • Keep the right balance of materials
  • Empty or maintain the bin regularly

A healthy worm bin usually smells earthy. A bokashi bin may have a sour fermented smell when opened, but it should not smell rotten.

How to Prevent Fruit Flies and Pests

Small indoor compost systems can attract fruit flies if scraps are exposed or the bin is poorly maintained.

To reduce the risk:

  • Keep food scraps covered
  • Bury scraps in bedding if using worms
  • Use a lid that fits well
  • Avoid leaving scraps out too long
  • Clean the area around the bin regularly
  • Freeze scraps if needed before adding them

A tidy system is much less likely to attract pests.

Where to Keep an Apartment Compost Bin

Indoor compost bins do not need much room. The best place is somewhere convenient but out of the way.

Common locations include:

  • Under the kitchen sink
  • In a laundry room
  • On a balcony if allowed
  • In a utility closet
  • In a corner of the kitchen

The ideal location depends on the method. Worm bins do best in a stable indoor temperature, while some sealed systems can also work on a balcony or patio.

Is Apartment Composting Worth It?

For many people, yes. Apartment composting is worth it because it turns waste into a useful resource and makes everyday living more sustainable.

It can be especially rewarding if you:

  • Grow herbs or houseplants
  • Garden on a balcony
  • Want to reduce food waste
  • Prefer eco-friendly habits
  • Have access to a community garden

Even if you do not use all the compost yourself, reducing waste is still a meaningful benefit.

Challenges of Composting in an Apartment

Apartment composting is possible, but it does come with a few challenges.

These may include:

  • Limited space
  • Concern about smell
  • Learning how to manage the system
  • Slower breakdown in very small bins
  • Deciding what to do with finished compost

Most of these challenges get easier once you build a simple routine.

Tips for Success

If you are new to apartment composting, start with a method that feels manageable.

A few good habits make a big difference:

  • Keep the system small and simple
  • Do not add too much at once
  • Learn what your method accepts
  • Store scraps neatly
  • Watch for moisture and smell changes
  • Stay consistent with maintenance

Indoor composting works best when it feels easy enough to keep doing.

Final Thoughts

You do not need a backyard to compost successfully. With the right setup, apartment composting can be simple, clean, and practical. Whether you choose a worm bin, bokashi bucket, or scrap collection system, there are plenty of ways to compost in a small space.

The best method is the one that fits your home and routine. Once you find that, composting in an apartment becomes much easier than many people expect.
